en.extract:
The speckled pigeon (Columba guinea), also known as the African rock pigeon or Guinea pigeon, is a pigeon that is a resident breeding bird in much of Africa south of the Sahara. It is a common and widespread species in open habitats over much of its range, although there are sizable gaps in its distribution.


Taxonomy
In 1747 the English naturalist George Edwards included a description and an illustration of the speckled pigeon in the second volume of his A Natural History of Uncommon Birds. His hand-coloured etching was made from two live birds at the home of the Duke of Richmond in London. Edwards was told that the pigeons had been brought from the inland region of Guinea in West Africa. When in 1758 the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the tenth edition, he placed the speckled pigeon with all the other pigeons in the genus Columba. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Columba guinea and cited Edwards' work.
Despite the marked differences in plumage from the rock dove C. livia, speckled pigeon is closely related to it, and in captivity will hybridise with it; the hybrid males (but not the first-generation hybrid females) are fertile. Some speckled pigeon ancestry is present in many breeds of domesticated pigeon, showing it has been used in pigeon breeding over a long period. However speckled pigeon genes are absent in natural wild rock dove populations.
There are two recognised subspecies:

C. g. guinea Linnaeus, 1758 – Mauritania to Ethiopia south to the Democratic Republic of the Congo and northern Malawi
C. g. phaeonota Gray, G.R., 1856 – southwestern Angola to Zimbabwe and South Africa


Description
It is a medium-sized pigeon, at 32–35 cm in length similar in size to rock dove and stock dove. Its back and wings are rufous, the latter heavily speckled with white spots. The rest of the upperparts and underparts are blue-grey, and the head is grey with red patches of bare skin around the eye. The neck is brownish, streaked with white, and the legs are red. The sexes are similar, but immatures are browner than adults and lack the red eye patches. The call is a loud doo-doo-doo.


Behaviour and ecology
The speckled pigeon is frequently seen around human habitation and cultivation. Most of its food is vegetable, and it gathers in large numbers where grain or groundnuts are available. This species builds a large stick nest on protected rocky outcrops and in urban areas often atop covered pergola pillars and on flat roofs under deep eaves and lays two white eggs. Its flight is quick, with regular beats and an occasional sharp flick of the wings that are characteristic of pigeons in general.


References


External links

(Rock Pigeon = ) Speckled Pigeon - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.
Speckled Pigeon: Breed Guide - Pigeonpedia
Speckled pigeon Structured guide to the species in southern Africa

The '''speckled pigeon''' ('''''Columba guinea'''''), also known as the '''African rock pigeon''' or '''Guinea pigeon''', is a [[Dove|pigeon]] that is a resident breeding bird in much of [[Africa]] south of the [[Sahara]]. It is a common and widespread species in open habitats over much of its range, although there are sizable gaps in its distribution.

==Taxonomy==
In 1747 the English naturalist [[George Edwards (naturalist)|George Edwards]] included a description and an illustration of the speckled pigeon in the second volume of his ''A Natural History of Uncommon Birds''. His hand-coloured etching was made from two live birds at the home of the [[Charles Lennox, 2nd Duke of Richmond|Duke of Richmond]] in London. Edwards was told that the pigeons had been brought from the inland region of [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]] in West Africa.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Edwards | first=George | author-link=George Edwards (naturalist) | year=1747 | title=A Natural History of Uncommon Birds | volume=Part II | place=London | publisher=Printed for the author, at the College of Physicians | page=75 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50240777 }}</ref> When in 1758 the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] updated his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' for the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]], he placed the speckled pigeon with all the other pigeons in the [[genus]] ''[[Columba (genus)|Columba]]''. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the [[binomial name]] ''Columba guinea'' and cited Edwards' work.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume= 1 | edition=10th | page=163 | publisher=Holmiae:Laurentii Salvii | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727070 }}</ref>

Despite the marked differences in plumage from the [[rock dove]] ''C. livia'', speckled pigeon is closely related to it, and in captivity will [[hybrid (biology)|hybridise]] with it; the hybrid males (but not the first-generation hybrid females) are fertile. Some speckled pigeon ancestry is present in many breeds of [[Domestic pigeon|domesticated pigeon]], showing it has been used in pigeon breeding over a long period. However speckled pigeon genes are absent in natural wild rock dove populations.<ref name="Vickrey">{{cite journal | last=Vickrey | first=Anna I | last2=Bruders | first2=Rebecca | last3=Kronenberg | first3=Zev | last4=Mackey | first4=Emma | last5=Bohlender | first5=Ryan J | last6=Maclary | first6=Emily T | last7=Maynez | first7=Raquel | last8=Osborne | first8=Edward J | last9=Johnson | first9=Kevin P | last10=Huff | first10=Chad D | last11=Yandell | first11=Mark | last12=Shapiro | first12=Michael D | title=Introgression of regulatory alleles and a missense coding mutation drive plumage pattern diversity in the rock pigeon | journal=eLife | volume=7 | date=2018-07-17 | issn=2050-084X | pmid=30014848 | pmc=6050045 | doi=10.7554/eLife.34803 | doi-access=free | page=}}</ref><ref name="Alonso">{{cite journal | last=Hernández-Alonso | first=Germán | last2=Ramos-Madrigal | first2=Jazmín | last3=van Grouw | first3=Hein | last4=Ciucani | first4=Marta Maria | last5=Cavill | first5=Emily Louisa | last6=Sinding | first6=Mikkel-Holger S | last7=Gopalakrishnan | first7=Shyam | last8=Pacheco | first8=George | last9=Gilbert | first9=M Thomas P | title=Redefining the Evolutionary History of the Rock Dove, ''Columba livia'', Using Whole Genome Sequences | journal=Molecular Biology and Evolution | volume=40 | issue=11 | date=2023-11-03 | issn=0737-4038 | pmid=37950889 | pmc=10667084 | doi=10.1093/molbev/msad243 | doi-access=free | url=https://academic.oup.com/mbe/advance-article-pdf/doi/10.1093/molbev/msad243/53252938/msad243.pdf | access-date=2025-05-24 | page=}}</ref>

There are two recognised [[subspecies]]:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date= July 2021 | title=Pigeons | work=IOC World Bird List Version 11.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pigeons/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 October 2021 }}</ref>
* ''C. g. guinea'' [[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]], 1758 – Mauritania to Ethiopia south to the Democratic Republic of the Congo and northern Malawi
* ''C. g. phaeonota'' [[George Robert Gray|Gray, G.R.]], 1856 – southwestern Angola to Zimbabwe and South Africa

==Description==
It is a medium-sized pigeon, at 32–35 cm in length similar in size to [[rock dove]] and [[stock dove]].<ref name="Hoyo">{{cite book | last=Hoyo | first=Josep del | title=All the Birds of the World | publisher=Lynx edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=2020 | isbn=978-84-16728-37-4 | page=85}}</ref> Its back and wings are rufous, the latter heavily speckled with white spots. The rest of the upperparts and underparts are blue-grey, and the head is grey with red patches of bare skin around the eye. The neck is brownish, streaked with white, and the legs are red. The sexes are similar, but immatures are browner than adults and lack the red eye patches. The call is a loud ''doo-doo-doo''.

==Behaviour and ecology==
The speckled pigeon is frequently seen around human habitation and cultivation. Most of its food is vegetable, and it gathers in large numbers where grain or groundnuts are available. This species builds a large stick nest on protected rocky outcrops and in urban areas often atop covered pergola pillars and on flat roofs under deep [[eaves]] and lays two white [[egg (biology)|eggs]]. Its flight is quick, with regular beats and an occasional sharp flick of the wings that are characteristic of pigeons in general.

zh.extract:
斑鸽（学名：Columba guinea）是鸽形目鸠鸽科鸽属的一种鸟类，广泛分布于撒哈拉以南非洲地区，是当地常见的鸽类之一。其外形特征明显，肩部密布白色斑点，形成醒目的斑纹；眼周具大片红色裸露皮肤，与灰色体羽形成鲜明对比。


特征
斑鸽体长约32至35厘米，体重219至390克。头部、胸部和腹部呈蓝灰色，臀部近银色；披肩及翅上覆羽为深红紫色。外侧翅覆羽呈蓝灰色，多数羽端具白色三角形斑点，双翅合拢时形成密集的白色斑纹。初级和次级飞羽为深板岩色。尾羽黑色，中央横贯一条淡灰色带，最外侧尾羽边缘颜色较浅。
颈部具由两叉状羽毛组成的颈环，羽基栗色，羽端呈银绿色或银粉色光泽。虹膜为白黄色、黄色或金色，外围具橙色或紫色环；腿和脚为粉红色、鲑鱼色或红色；喙黑色，鼻孔周围为白色。
雌雄外形相近。幼鸟整体羽色较为暗淡，翅部白斑不明显，颈部羽毛不具成鸟特有的两叉状结构，脚和眼周裸露皮肤呈灰白色。
